data.title: July Jobs Report: Revisions Show Weakness
data.description:
Introduction

I've been wary of the U.S. labor market's health lately, writing that there are "cracks beneath the headlines," and that we shouldn't "take the jobs report at face value." While I may sound gloomy on the subject, I am optimistic about the economy as a whole. I just believe that if we do see a real economic slowdown in our current cycle, the labor market will be the canary in the coal mine.

The headline, like most months, is that the U.S. added jobs. This month, the figure is at 73,000 jobs added. The twist?

Data from May and June were revised down, shaving off 258,000 jobs from the two months.

This means that the average gain of 150,000 jobs/month over the last three months, from May to July, was cut down to 35,000 jobs/month — this is a hugely different figure, and should raise alarm for folks watching the labor market closely. These kinds of deep revisions mean that our assumptions of strength based on the last few months of data need to be re-evaluated.

The July Jobs Report

Most jobs added came in private education and health services with the largest losses in professional services, manufacturing, and government.

The headline unemployment rate is back at 4.2%, still in the range it's been in for the last year, and up from last month's 4.1%. This doesn't establish any kind of trend, and for now, it seems we will continue to stay in this range unless conditions get materially worse. There seems to be little catalyst for the rate to drop so long as Fed interest rates remain restrictive and the global trade war remains ongoing. Both of those leave too much uncertainty on the table for businesses to go on hiring sprees.

Note how low the last two months look now that they've been revised down so heavily; June and May 2025 were the worst months for job creation in the last two years.

Beyond these figures, I have also been closely watching the "long-term unemployed rate," or those unemployed for more than 27 weeks. This figure rose by 179,000 people, totaling 1.8M Americans. This figure has been growing steadily over the last year, up from 1.5M in July 2024. It now represents a quarter of all unemployed folks.

I've highlighted that stat below, but look at the other durations as well. Remember that folks move from one category to another over time, as they remain unemployed for longer, so there is some "double counting" going on between the figures.

I want to focus on a statistic that doesn't show up in the unemployment rate, which I feel is often overlooked because of that reason: folks out of the labor force entirely. This figure is made up of people who don't want to, have to, or cannot afford to work. While marginally attached people (part-time workers who want full-time work) and discouraged workers figures lowered, the total figure of folks not in the labor force increased MoM and YoY, leading to a lower overall participation rate.

When looking at the sectoral gains and losses, we see a mixed bag. Like the last few months, the largest gain was in private education and healthcare employment.

We also saw a jump in retail trade, partially correcting a downtrend. Professional services saw a continued decline, as did manufacturing — these two are concerning trends to see continue through the last few months. Future jobs data showing these trends snapping would be very bullish for labor markets, especially manufacturing, as it is one of the core priorities of the current administration.

One thing to note is that we are unlikely to see healthcare jobs decline anytime soon, and that may be one major point of strength. Healthcare jobs are likely to continue keeping the numbers up, especially as the U.S. is facing an expected shortage of healthcare workers across the board.

This map is emblematic of the larger economy shifting and transforming, underpinned not just by the last few months of this kind of data, but the last few decades. In 1990, the largest employers in most states were manufacturers, while today it's healthcare almost across the board. This is what a "maintenance economy" looks like, where our job growth is being driven by demographic changes and pushing more and more people into care service roles.

The Fed Is More Likely To Act

The revisions over the past few months paint the labor market as far weaker than initially expected and dampens my outlook on the economy. I don't believe that we're in some spiral or horrific place — please don't get me wrong here. However, the labor market is weaker than expected, and one way to address the issue is for the Fed to loosen its policy stance.

We're already starting to see dissent within the FOMC, a rare sight as most of their actions are unanimous.

But labor market conditions are often cited as a reason to hold rates in the current "moderately restrictive" 4.25-4.50% range. One of the dissenting governors, Waller, cited the labor market as his reason to vote for a cut at the July meeting.

The next meeting isn't until September, and while the market was expecting rates to be held at that meeting, which will come after another round of jobs data in August, and two CPI readings, they are now widely anticipating rate cuts. The futures market shifted from a 62.3% chance of no cut in September to an 83.4% chance of a 25bp cut based on this report — that is the change over one day. It's a big swing in the futures market, but it's reacting to the large swing in jobs data now that we are seeing such deep revisions to the past two months.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I still see the Fed cutting in September, which I believed before this round of data as well. This only reinforces the need for the Fed to loosen its rate policy, especially if they are not looking to reduce the pace of their asset roll-off. I am hesitant to say that we could see further cuts this year; we may only see the one, but I do think it comes in September based on this data.

Will it stop the assault on Powell and the FOMC at large from the White House? Unlikely. President Trump believes rates should be lowered by the full point range, and yesterday. A September cut will not stop the drama, but it could bring some economic relief.

The Fed may finally be done with its "wait and see" approach by their next meeting, especially if the next round of jobs data for August is similar to July's. If patterns continue, especially around long-term unemployment, prime-age discouraged workers, and the labor force participation rate, we will need cuts in the rate to loosen up economic conditions.

That meeting won't happen for a while, so for now, it's best for investors to wait and see and act carefully. Markets are down on the news of the jobs report, but it may just be a temporary reaction; they often are. The news cycle will move on quickly, so long as we don't see the same deep revisions next month that we did this month.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Shoulder Innovations: Interesting, Not Convinced
data.description:
Shares of Shoulder Innovations (NYSE:SI) have seen a disappointing public offering, with shares pricing 25% below the offer price, and trading dead flat around that number after the first day of trading.

The company aims to provide better shoulder surgical care and despite growing revenues and modest sales multiples, investors are not sold on the idea. Perhaps, this is the result of the pace of growth is slowing down rather dramatically in recent quarters.

This makes shares interesting as an implied low sales multiple applied to the business, yet I am not yet putting my shoulders under this offering here, yet keen to keep track of the developments from here, post the offering.

Transforming Shoulder Surgical Care

Shoulder Innovations is a business, which the name suggests, focuses solely on transforming the shoulder surgical care market. The company offers advanced implant systems for shoulder arthroplasty. By offering a complete suite of products and services: that of preparation planning, implant design and overall procedure efficiency, in order to deliver on superior outcomes for patients and other shareholders.

Shoulder pain in itself is both chronic and widely prevalent, having a wide range of sources which includes osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, shoulder fracture and also so-called rotator cuff tears. Nearly 10-year-old data suggests that 8 million physician visits in the US have been tied to such problems.

The company's initial focus in this market is on shoulder arthroplasty, which is an established surgical procedure which involves reconstruction of the shoulder joint with prosthetic implants. This can be done through an anatomic total shoulder arthroplasty (aTSA) or a reverse total shoulder arthroplasty (rTSA). These procedures can be performed in impatient hospital settings but also so-called ambulatory surgery centers, the latter of which is most cost-effective.

Traditional implants often have limitations which relate to poor mechanical fit, or less than ideal kinematics, imprecise planning and nonspecialized support. This might result in continued pain for patients, but also less mobility and implants being removed, adding to patient comfort and healthcare costs.

The company has developed its products to cater both procedure types, that is aTSA and rTSA. Solutions focus on preparation, addressing the shortfall of traditional procedures, this starting with the ProVoyance surgery planning technology which was cleared by the FDA in 2021.

Valuation And IPO Thoughts

Shoulder Innovations aimed to sell 5 million shares in a preliminary price range between $19 and $21 per share, with final pricing set far below the range at $15 per share. The company raised some $75 million in gross proceeds from the offering, which excludes money obtained by a potential overallotment option.

Nearly 20 million shares are outstanding following the offering, which grants the business a $300 million equity valuation at the offer price, a number which actually includes a roughly $150 million net cash position.

The company generated $19.3 million in revenues from selling these solutions in 2023 accompanied by substantial operating loss of $10.9 million. Revenues rose by no less than 63% in 2024 to $31.6 million, as operating losses increased to $14.7 million which is up in absolute terms, yet relative losses were coming down. These revenues were generated from 4,349 systems being sold, suggesting average prices per unit of just over $7,000.

Growth is slowing down with first quarter sales for 2025 up just over 40% to $10.1 million, with revenues trending at $40 million per annum already. Operating losses of $4.3 million keep increasing in dollar terms, but gradually improved in relative terms, this number is up a million from the year before. Preliminary second quarter sales are seen at a midpoint of $11.0 million, suggesting that growth slows down to 32% on an annual basis.

With shares trading at the $15 mark on the first day of trading, shares are clearly defended by its underwriters here. As a result, the valuation discussion above remains intact. Frankly the business has a lot of cash which is needed given the continued losses here, seen trending around $20 million per annum, although that interest received on net cash holdings might lower this loss rate a bit here.

A $150 million operating asset valuation values the business at 3-4 times sales, which frankly is not too high for a medical device business, certainly one showing rapid growth, although that the pace of annual sales growth is taking a real beating in recent quarters, with the annual growth rate of more than 60% for all of 2024 seen cut in half in the second quarter of this year already.

Concluding Thoughts

The valuation discussion above is quite interesting, as this is a commercial medical device business with real and growing revenues, yet unfortunately also losses. The biggest risks in my eyes is not near term profitability (or lack thereof), given the net cash holdings, but really concentration of the product suite.

This of course creates commercial reliance risks, but also the risks within a regulatory framework. Other risks include the rapid pace at which growth is slowing down, and mostly related to this, the competitive situation versus the solutions offered by its peers.

This includes major peers like Johnson & Johnson, Nephew, Stryker, Zimmer Biomet, to name a few, which provided opportunities for M&A as well if these players like the technology. After all, such capital-rich peers can buy a company like Shoulders, while gaining scale distribution advantages given their scale.

Weighing it all others, I am performing a balancing act, as I am concluding to be cautious given the slower pace of growth here, which for now is the most important. Operating leverage is nice to see, but not key at this point in time, also given the substantial net cash holdings. However, implied revenue multiples are quite modest, also given the poor pricing action since the public offering, which provides an opportunity, but for that I am looking for revenue growth to stabilize, or even pick up from here.

data.title: Capgemini: Signs Of Recovery Not Enough To Offset Near-Term Weakness
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Capgemini (OTCPK:CAPMF) in Feb’25, in which I recommended a hold rating as I was disappointed by the absence of growth acceleration and that the demand backdrop was weaker than expected, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. While green shoots have turned into more credible signs of recovery in North America and the UK, core challenges, especially in Continental Europe and manufacturing, remain unresolved. With GenAI momentum building but near-term visibility still murky, my view on CAPMF remains unchanged at hold.

2Q25 Earnings Review

1H25 revenue grew 0.2% y/y at constant currency to EUR11.1 billion, with 2Q25 showing a sequential acceleration to 0.7% y/y, which was a 110 bps improvement from 1Q25. Regionally, the recovery was mixed. North America reaccelerated to 2.4% y/y, helped by strength in financial services and TMT, while the UK & Ireland grew 8% y/y. However, France remained deeply negative at -5%, and the rest of Europe declined 2.3%, highlighting continued weakness in Continental Europe. Sector-wise, Financial Services and TMT led with 5.5% y/y growth, while Manufacturing stayed as a drag at -4%, albeit improving sequentially. Nonetheless, the accelerating momentum vs. 1Q25 prompted management to tighten FY25 guidance to -1% to 1%, up from the previous -2% to 2% range. Margins held up well. 1H25 EBIT margin was flat y/y at 12.4%, supported by SG&A discipline as gross margin dipped 30 bps. Lastly, adj. EPS grew modestly by 2% y/y to EUR6.

Reasons to turn bullish

What I saw as non-convincing data points that there were early signs of stabilization in North America and the UK have now turned into much stronger evidence. North America, which I was skeptical of last quarter, showed a clear inflection with 2.4% y/y growth, and it seems plausible that this momentum can continue given the signal that IT spending is likely bottoming out here (as seen from the continued acceleration in TMT sectors). Meanwhile, the UK & Ireland revenue growth accelerated 400 bps y/y to 8% y/y, marking its strongest growth in several quarters. While these are being offset by the persistent weakness in Continental Europe, this does suggest that CAPMF's localized execution strategy is paying off. This strategy was introduced in 3Q24. The idea is to focus on adapting workforce deployment to localized needs (adding offshore capacity in recovering markets like the US while rightsizing in underperformers like France).

There is also growing traction in GenAI, which I had previously viewed as too early to drive material results. That narrative has shifted. Management disclosed that GenAI and Agentic AI contributed >7% of total bookings in 2Q25, up from 5% in 4Q24. They even explicitly said that clients are actively reallocating discretionary IT budgets toward GenAI initiatives, which is a big takeaway given the broader macro weakness. This accelerating reallocation trend changes things. If the direction is right, that AI demand will continue to scale up, and the pace of acceleration should increase. CAPMF adds ~1 pt of acceleration per quarter now (4Q24 was 5%), and if we assume this accelerates to ~3 pts by the end of the year, it implies GenAI as a percentage of total bookings to reach ~17% and be on track to reach >20% by 2027. The top-line growth acceleration implication here is that GenAI would accelerate it as it becomes a larger portion of the mix.

The WNS acquisition is also starting to make more strategic sense in this context. While this was initially perceived as a standard business process outsourcing [BPO] play by the market, I thought management had laid out a solid case as to why that is not the case in the 2Q25 call. They framed it as a bridge to building scaled capabilities in what they call “Agentic AI-powered Intelligent Operations.” The goal is to move away from labor-based BPO toward AI-enabled business outcome delivery. If this is done successfully, my belief is that it could meaningfully expand CAPMF’s addressable market and open doors to larger, transformational outsourcing deals. The CEO even highlighted the potential for "mega deals" emerging from this repositioning. And if true, it could become a new growth engine and help support the long-term bull case.

Reasons to be bearish

There are reasons to not be bullish about CAPMF, and readers should be aware. Continental Europe continues to be the clear weak link. In my last review, I called out France as a major drag, and the latest -5% y/y decline in 2Q25 confirms that weakness still persists. Management’s comment also doesn’t instill any confidence, as they noted broad weakness in France and no near-term signs of recovery. The broader Rest of Europe segment also stayed negative at -2.3% y/y, reinforcing the narrative that Europe remains a real overhang on consolidated growth. Given that Europe still accounts for a significant portion of the CAPMF revenue base, this continuous drag limits the potential for a full-fledged recovery and continues to dilute momentum from North America and the UK. Also, while Financials and TMT did well, the Manufacturing vertical fell again, by 4% y/y in 2Q25, and since it represents more than a quarter of CAPMF group revenue, this creates a sizeable drag.

Moreover, despite sounding confident in the GenAI story, I sensed uncertainty in management’s tone. They did raise guidance slightly, but the overall message remained cautious. They specifically noted there is “too much uncertainty” to have conviction in how 4Q25 will shape up, despite expectations for 3Q25 to look similar to 2Q25. I don’t blame them for being conservative, but this really shows how volatile the macro situation is and that it continues to cloud visibility.

Thank you. Good question. Of course, as you know, we are pretty cautious because we know changing the environment can be pretty brutal. We, for the moment, see some stability going in Q3. So we can expect for the moment Q3 to be similar to Q2. That's what we see. So of course it all depends after that of what the environment looks like going into Q4. But as I say, they see too much uncertainty to be able to really be sure about how Q4 looks like. And that's why we're going to remain cautious from that perspective. 2Q25 call

Valuation

CAPMF valuation has seen a steep correction since February (in hindsight, shifting away from a buy rating was right), down from 14x to 11x forward PE today. It may seem “overly beaten” to some, but I can see the merits of why the market punished the stock. The bullish points I made above support the mid-to-longer bull case (even if I’m right, GenAI bookings impact will only be apparent in FY26 and beyond), but the bearish points are immediately visible today, clearly weighing on growth. Until CAPMF can fix these near-term issues, especially in Europe and the manufacturing vertical, I really don’t see a strong reason for multiples to trade any higher.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for CAPMF is hold. The recovery in North America and increasing GenAI contributions are promising, but the fundamental issues in Continental Europe and manufacturing continue to weigh on the near-term outlook. At 11x forward earnings, the stock doesn’t look expensive, but I don’t see a clear catalyst for rerating until there’s more evidence of broad-based growth reacceleration.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Shake Shack: Sitting On Gains, Watching For The Next Dip
data.description:
It looks like we nailed the bottom on this one. If you scooped up Shake Shack (NYSE:SHAK) in the $80s or $90s, you're probably sitting on gains of +30%.

That's basically how we played the April dip—loading up on high-growth restaurant names like Portillo's (PTLO) and Kura Sushi (KRUS). But let's be clear: some of those are still trading at a discount, and this is definitely not the case with Shake Shack.

If you take a look at my last article about this company and scroll down to the comments section, you'll notice that I flagged Shake Shack stock as a 'Hold' back in May.

Check the chart; you'll see the stock was trading in the low $120s at that point—easily beating my $110 price target by at least $10.

Since I'm confident Shake Shack will keep expanding across the U.S. over the next decade—though not without facing challenges like pricing power, suburban adaptation, and the late rollout of combo meals—the smartest move is to accumulate shares during the dips.

Shake Shack probably won't ever look 'cheap' on multiples, but that doesn't mean you should pay up when it's trading in the triple digits.

Stay patient, and the market will likely hand you another opportunity.

And in my view, maybe—just maybe—a little post-earnings multiple compression could bring us what we've been waiting for: Shake Shack dipping back below $100!

Either way, the stock is already down more than 10% before most people have had their morning coffee this beautiful Thursday. It's not quite at my target yet, but it's a start.

That said, I have two goals for this follow-up:

  1. Give some color on what Q2 FY 2025 was like;
  2. Understand how the improved guidance will influence my $110 price target.

This last one is what really makes my head itch.

A Busy Shack In Q2

Bucking the downward trends seen across many QSRs and benefiting from New York's recovery, Shake Shack came through with another double beat. Aside from Q1 FY 2025—when the company posted a rare double miss—this high-growth fast-casual chain has built an impressive track record: eight EPS beats and two in-line results over the last ten quarters.

Q2 FY 2025 followed this logic: EPS of $0.41 (beating Wall St. estimates by at least $0.03) and revenue of $356.5 (beating by $2.3 million).

But before we get into the specifics, let's provide a little more context.

Although Placer.ai didn't identify these recovery trends in the NY-NJ area, Black Box Intelligence reported that it was one of the 'Best Performers' in both May and June. This contrasted with California, which was the worst performing market since the beginning of the year (excluding February).

As you well know, all the data on the table points to a sequential recovery in the industry from March to June—indicating a relatively strong Q2, especially for casual dining.

However, sunshine isn't for everyone.

For QSRs overall—both fast food and fast casual—the pace has clearly slowed, as highlighted by RMS data. To put it in perspective, QSR traffic saw a sequential drop, landing at a 0.9% year-over-year decline, marking the worst performance since Q4 FY 2024. So, we continue to see this value gap transforming into a traffic gap between QSRs and FSRs as the latter leaned toward prix fixe promotions and early-bird dining.

What's impressive is how Shake Shack has managed to sidestep some of those headwinds.

Thanks to its premium positioning, smart footprint in high-DMA (and more resilient) markets, and ongoing menu innovation, it's stayed ahead of the curve when others are slowing down.

It's easy to see this resilience in other QSRs. Just look at Yum Brands' (YUM) darling, Taco Bell. Using its 'Innovation Wheel' and combo meals, the restaurant chain not only sidestepped the 'Better Value Than Fast Food' movement but also the weakness of Mexican concepts!

Take New York as an example.

According to data from Placer.ai, while fast-food peers like Burger King (QSR), McDonald's (MCD), and Wendy's (WEN) saw negative year-over-year traffic in every month of Q2 (except for McDonald's in May), higher value concepts like Taco Bell outperform the state's QSR market.

So you see, it's not just about the price tag; it's about the value packed into every dollar.

Sure, price sensitivity hits harder in the suburbs, but even there, folks recognize when spending a bit more gets them a tastier, more satisfying meal.

Starting from the top, Shake Shack reported revenue of $356.5 million in the quarter (up 12.6% year-over-year), driven by the opening of new Shacks (licensed and company-owned) and same-store sales growth of 1.8% year-over-year.

Let's delve deeper into each of these drivers.

Starting with same-store sales, Shake Shack accelerated them compared to Q1 FY 2025 (remember, the quarter with snowstorms, a terrible February, and other regional factors) by at least 160 bp sequentially—thanks to a boost in traffic.

We should also put into perspective that in the last eight quarters, Shake Shack has not reported at least one period of negative comparable sales year-over-year. This tells us that comparisons are becoming increasingly fraught.

Just take a look at how this compares to other QSRs that have already reported Q2 FY 2025 results:

During the quarter, they also opened 13 new company-owned Shacks (two of them with drive-thrus, contemplating expansion beyond cities) and nine licensed Shacks, almost all international.

This brought the number of Shacks in operation to 611 worldwide. Of these, 346 are company-owned and 265 licensed.

Looking back, in Q2 FY 2024, Shake Shack had 547 restaurants systemwide (311 company-owned and 236 licensed). This implies a growth rate of approximately 11.7% year-over-year, in line with their long-term goal of low-teens unit growth.

It's interesting to observe the path of this growth quarter-by-quarter:

But you know what might have been even more interesting than the growth story this quarter? Shacks are becoming more efficient—great news for margins.

In Q2 FY 2025, the Shack-level margin was 23.9% (above the FY 2025 guidance of 23% to 23.5%). Compare it to other high-growth fast-casuals, and you'll see that this is in the top quartile. Examples? They're right here: Portillo's (18% to 20% depending on the location), Potbelly (PBPB) (around 16% to 17%), and El Pollo Loco (LOCO) (16% to 18%).

The improvements are coming precisely where prices are stickiest: labor.

The rollout of kiosks across the system last year made a real difference—it helped cut down lines, reduce churn, raise the average check per guest, and, best of all, ease labor costs. Speaking of which, they decreased from 28.4% of revenue to 25.7% in Q2 FY 2025. Meanwhile, Food, Beverage, and Packaging increased 40 bp year over year to 28.2%.

All in all, both figures fall comfortably within the bounds of the classic '30-30-30 Rule,' so there's really nothing to raise an eyebrow at.

But you know what's most interesting when we look at these numbers?

If we take into account AUVs of approximately $4 million and this restaurant-level margin of 23.9%, we would get a three-year payback period for the new Shacks. This is the same payback as Portillo's locations in Chicagoland and also matches that of Potbelly's new savings account of just $650,000!

All this bottom line growth boosted EBITDA to $58.9 million, up 24.8% year-over-year.

Net income also grew 77.8% year-over-year to $18.5 million, raising diluted EPS from $0.23 to $0.41. Again, very strong 91.3% year-over-year growth!

Adding it all up, we have:

  1. Low single-digit year-over-year same-store sales growth driven by strong NY footprint; Shacks' double-digit growth continues;
  2. Improvement in Shack-level margins, above guidance for the full year of FY 2025;
  3. Self-financing growth (given the $24 million increase in cash and only $1 million in long-term debt);
  4. Double-digit growth in metrics such as EBITDA, EBIT, Net Income, and EPS.

Honestly, if Shake Shack were trading at slightly less stretched multiples, we'd be seeing the stock soar.

Valuation seems to have spoken louder. However, this correction seems healthy to me. After all, I had pointed out that Shake Shack had overextended itself in May and continued to do so in subsequent months.

Now, I just have to figure out: At $120, does Shake Shack still offer a buying opportunity, or is it better to wait for another bottom below $100?

To Buy, or Not to Buy—That Is the Question

Looking at the entire FY 2025, management raised EBITDA to between $210 and $220 million but maintained revenue expectations at between $1.4 and $1.5 billion.

From this, we can deduce that the EBITDA margin they're expecting is somewhere around 15% at the midpoint, well above the five-year average (6.94%) and last year's (11.17%). Now, if the EBITDA margin has improved significantly, we can also expect stronger net income, right?

The analyst consensus expects EPS of $1.36 for the entire FY 2025. Note that this would represent an incredibly strong increase compared to FY 2024's EPS of $0.65 (at least 109% year-over-year, to be precise).

Is this possible? Putting into perspective about 40 million shares outstanding, we'd be talking about a net profit of $54.4 million for FY 2025. Note that the margin implied by this projection would be 3.75%, close to those of FY 2018 and FY 2019. So, it seems achievable to me.

So, it's only fair to take another look at the valuation.

Starting with the peers—which one to use?

For Shake Shack, I'm looking at other high-growth fast-casual restaurants with mixed ownership (franchisee and company-owned). I'm talking about companies like:

  • Chipotle (CMG);
  • Sweetgreen (SG).

We have revenue, EBITDA, and EPS projections for the entire FY 2025, so we'll use those.

Starting with revenue and excluding outliers, we arrive at an EV/SV multiple of approximately 3.7x. For Shake Shack, I'm using 42 million shares outstanding and Shake Shack's current capital structure.

This would give us a price target of $125 to $135 ($130 at the midpoint) using the revenue guidance of $1.4 to $1.5 billion.

For P/S, we can obtain an average multiple of 3.4x. The RPS falls within the range of $33.33 to $35.71.

This indicates a price target of $115 to $124.

Moving to multiples such as EV/EBITDA and excluding outliers, we could find a multiple of 25.3x.

Based on the guided range of $210 to $220 million, we would arrive at a price target of $129 to $135.

Combining this, we arrive at the following scenario using the midpoint of all metrics:

Based on my updated price target of $127 to $130, we're looking at a potential upside of around 5.8% to 8.3% from current levels. It's not exactly screaming cheap, but after today's selloff, it makes a solid case for a rebound back into that range.

All things considered, I consider Shake Shack stock a 'Hold' today, even with my newly improved price target, and I'll explain why.

First off, shares are now hovering near fair value. Any upside from here would likely be in the single digits—not exactly compelling at these current multiples. But if you followed my call back in April or May, you're likely sitting on gains north of 30% and holding a solid average cost from a long-term perspective.

So, from that perspective, there's no reason to be so greedy here with this tiny upside.

Second, it seems that over the course of the quarter, the market may have already priced in the improvements in guidance (including in margins at the Shack level) and even exceeded them a bit, pushing it into overvalued territory.

Today's drop was a healing move. Not only did it bring Shake Shack closer to its fair value, but it also gave us a more optimistic outlook for FY 2025 as a whole.

My third point here is that the sector's recovery is still fragile, and a macro-driven multiple compression could still occur.

In other words, another 'April bottom' could still occur. If it does, we need to take advantage again.

Looking at a base case—where Shake Shack keeps trading at elevated multiples but dips back below my target—I'd see the low $110s as a solid entry point. At that level, it would definitely flip back to a 'Buy' in my book.

But if we get a macro-driven pullback-like the one in April—that drags the stock below $100, the setup changes completely. In that scenario, I'd likely upgrade my rating to a 'Strong Buy,' as the risk-reward would turn significantly more asymmetric.

data.title: Franklin Total Return Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

  • The fund’s exposure to HY corporate bonds contributed to relative performance, as did overweight allocations to IG corporate bonds, senior secured floating-rate bank loans and CLOs. Conversely, security selection in IG corporate bonds hindered results. During the quarter, we increased our allocation to IG corporate bonds. We trimmed our exposure to HY corporate bonds, senior secured floating-rate bank loans and CLOs.
  • US duration positioning contributed to results.
  • Overweight allocations to MBS and RMBS contributed to results. We maintained our MBS allocation while increasing our RMBS exposure.

Outlook

  • In our view, the Fed is unlikely to cut interest rates soon. Despite recent signs of cooling inflation, Fed officials remain cautious due to ongoing tariff-related uncertainties. Companies are expected to raise prices as pre-tariff inventories diminish, leading to a likely rise in core goods inflation. Furthermore, average US tariff rates remain historically high despite a temporary easing in US-China trade tensions. We therefore expect potentially higher inflation and slower growth. With inflation still above target and risks skewed to the upside, we expect the Fed to maintain its pause. Amid still-resilient industrial production and retail sales, we believe the Fed is likely to stay on hold until there is clear evidence of labor market deterioration without a simultaneous rise in inflation.
  • The "One Big Beautiful Bill" spending package will further increase the US deficit over the next decade. Combined with factors such as uncertain trade policy (which has raised stagflationary concerns) and an expansionary fiscal policy (which implies greater debt issuance), we expect longer-term rates to continue to climb higher through the rest of the year.
  • For the corporate credit sectors, we maintain our emphasis on diligent security selection and individual company fundamentals. IG credit fundamentals have remained supportive. While we believe IG corporate bonds continue to offer a relatively attractive yield, spread levels offer little cushion for further economic, market, or geopolitical surprises. In our view, spreads are likely to remain in a trading range but, at current levels, are more likely to widen than tighten. In the lower-credit-quality HY and floating-rate bank loan sectors, although we remain broadly constructive amid generally supportive technical conditions, we continue to favor quality companies amid persistent market bifurcation and idiosyncratic opportunities as we evaluate each company’s fundamentals and exposure to different possible policy alternatives.
  • For securitized sectors, our generally neutral stance remains largely unchanged. Prepayment risk remains minimal, and mortgage credit quality remains high. The fundamental and technical outlook for the sector remains favorable, with attractive relative value to IG corporates. We also anticipate increased demand from banks if the yield curve normalizes or regulatory uncertainty dissipates. For RMBS, heightened macroeconomic uncertainty due to the Trump administration’s fiscal and foreign policies and tense geopolitical climate could result in more periods of elevated rate volatility and “risk-off” episodes. We therefore prefer a defensive positioning at the top of capital stack in higher carry profile bonds with limited extension risk. Despite our generally cautious stance, we are finding value and identifying opportunities in higher-quality fixed income investments.

data.title: The AI Boom Vs. The Economic 'Kryptonite' Of High-Interest Rates And Tariffs
data.description:
On July 30, the Federal Reserve announced the decision to keep interest rates unchanged at its Federal Open Market Committee, or "FOMC" meeting for July. The stock market declined after this decision, even though most market participants were not really expecting a rate cut. Part of the disappointment might have been due to some hawkish comments that came from this event, which seem to call into question the potential for a rate cut during the next FOMC meeting, which is in September.

In addition to interest rate policy concerns, investors have also been dealing with tariff policies from the Trump Administration, which seems to be a bit like playing "Whack-A-Mole," since as soon as tariff policies seem to be resolved for one country or one sector, another issue comes up the next day or week. Aside from the rapid plunge that the stock market experienced in April over Liberation Day tariffs, the market now seems immune (for the time being) to tariff concerns. However, in terms of tariffs and higher for longer interest rate policies, I think the downside risks for the economy and the stock market are growing, even as the stock market seems to be looking past these issues as it hits new record highs in recent days.

I believe a lot of the bullishness is being driven by the AI boom, and it appears that this is what investors are focusing on, and that is what could be masking signs of weakness in the real economy and signs of poor price action in many economically sensitive stocks. While I am bullish about AI and humanoid robots and the future in general, I feel there is an increasing divergence between the "Magnificent 7" and the rest of the market, which is not doing nearly as well. If investors did not have AI to focus on right now, I think it's clear the market would not be at record levels. I also believe that Jerome Powell's hawkish and higher for longer interest rate policies and President Trump's high tariff policies are going to turn out to be "Kryptonite" for the U.S. and the global economy if these policies continue for much longer.

So for me, the question becomes how far can the AI boom carry the economy even though it does not appear to be boosting much of anything other than tech companies and their stocks. The other questions are: how much longer can the economy support a higher for longer interest rate policy before something breaks, and can the global economy withstand the massive increase in U.S. tariffs? Historical precedence shows that high-interest rates and high tariffs are basically economic kryptonite, so the longer this continues, the more risk we will have of a big market correction and/or recession.

The Market Is Overbought

When I see the chart of the S&P 500 Index (NYSEARCA:SPY) below, it sure looks overbought and ripe for a pullback:

Powell's Rate Policy

A couple of things are worth noting when it comes to the July FOMC meeting. There were two governors that dissented, who were Christopher Waller and Michelle Bowman, who voted for a 0.25% rate cut. While most investors did not expect a rate cut in July, I think some of Jerome Powell's comments were not viewed as being positive for rate cuts during the next FOMC meeting in September. He even brought up the idea of raising rates. In regards to the inflation he believes might be coming from high tariffs, Jerome Powell said: "You could argue we are a bit looking through goods inflation by not raising rates." He also made comments that we are very early on in the process of the new tariffs when it comes to the potential impact on inflation. It's also worth noting that in the FOMC comments, the Fed's target rate of 2% was discussed. In view of the fact that it is currently around 2.8%, a decline in inflation could have a long way to go, especially if tariffs boost inflation in the coming months. This brings up the question of whether the Fed needs to abandon its target goal of a 2% inflation rate, and I believe the answer is yes.

President Trump's Tariff Policies

I believe that the full impact of potential tariff policy damage is still coming. It is just too early for investors to believe that the all-clear signal is here. Many companies stocked up on raw materials and inventory during Q2 and have been able to hold off on price increases for this reason. So big price increases are likely still to come, and that could really dampen consumer confidence and spending in the months ahead. In addition, many consumers appear to have bought items that they did not need right away in order to get "pre-tariff" deals on many items. This could have boosted consumer spending and given the economy a temporary surge from this pull forward in demand. The problem is that this could create a drastic drop in the coming quarters when it comes to consumer spending, especially if prices jump fairly soon.

Here is where things stand with some of the major trading partners:

Europe:

In many ways, it looks like President Trump is gaining an edge when it comes to negotiating trade deals with many countries. It seems like the United States was deemed a winner when it comes to the recently announced trade deal with Europe, whereby it agreed to accept a new 15% tariff on most goods and buy various products from the U.S. over the next few years. By contrast, it looks like the U.S. did not make any concessions.

China:

There is currently a 30% tariff on Chinese goods coming into the U.S. and a 10% tariff on U.S. goods going into China. However, this is part of a tariff truce that is set to expire on August 12, although it could be extended.

India:

President Trump recently announced a 25% tariff on India and said that an additional "penalty" will be added soon because India is buying weapons and oil from Russia.

In addition to these and many other countries, President Trump has announced a new 50% tariff on copper, and he announced a 40% tariff on all transshipped goods. In addition, he has said he would put tariffs on semiconductors and pharmaceuticals in the near future.

Signs Of Economic Weakness And Other Concerns

In recent earnings reports, a number of bellwether companies, such as UPS (UPS) said that they saw signs of weakness, which in part were due to tariffs. Whirlpool (WHR) and Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) also reported that tariffs were having a significant impact on their businesses. In a truly vibrant economy, I think we would be seeing package volumes going up for UPS and that appliance and tool sales would be strong for companies like Whirlpool and Stanley Black & Decker, but that is not the case, and I see this as a warning sign.

There are signs that the labor market is softening, and this is often an early warning sign of more weakness to come. Recent data shows that new job openings have hit the lowest levels in many months, and this could mean softer data will show up in the coming quarters. While AI has created a massive spending boom for advanced chips and other data center equipment, it seems like AI is already creating job losses, especially in tech companies that are using AI to do coding and other tasks. In the past couple of months, companies like Microsoft (MSFT), Autodesk (ADSK), and Alphabet (GOOG), (GOOGL), have announced layoffs.

The Federal Reserve suggests it is data dependent, which to me already implies that it could always be too late because by the time the data shows rate cuts are needed, it is often because the economy is already in a recession. Historically, the Federal Reserve has underestimated the increase in unemployment by 2.5%. This potentially means that the unemployment rate will get considerably worse from the 4.2% level it holds now. The Fed is predicting the unemployment rate will reach 4.5% in 2026, but if history repeats and unemployment is 2.5% higher than Fed projections, that means it could hit about 7%. This could cause a significant correction in the stock market.

Where I Could Be Wrong

The AI boom could continue to be a huge driver of economic growth, and it is possible that after a rough patch, many companies will find a way to absorb the tariffs into their business model without too much harm to the consumer. It is also possible that tariffs will come down in the future or even potentially be struck down by the courts as being an illegal use of presidential power since there are legal challenges facing President Trump's tariff policies. I have to wonder if perhaps Europe is counting on this being the case, as it would explain why they accepted a seemingly one-sided deal without much fight. Perhaps the tariffs will be transitory, and perhaps the Fed will cut rates later this year and more so after May 2026, which is when Jerome Powell's term as Fed Chair expires. If this happens, the AI boom could continue and the economic growth could broaden out for the economy with lower interest rates.

In Summary

Right now I see AI boosting investor sentiment with the stock market at or near record levels. But, under the surface, there is real weakness in areas that should be strong if the economy is solid and seeing broad growth, but that is not the case right now. In the meantime, I believe the higher for longer interest rate policy and Trump's high tariffs are going to act like economic Kryptonite if these two policies are kept in place for much longer. For these reasons, I think something is going to have to give, because if we continue on this path, I believe it will lead to a recession due to job losses and reduced consumer confidence and spending in the coming months. With the stock market trading at historically high levels, I think it is time to lock in some gains to be sure there is plenty of cash to buy during the next potential market correction.

data.title: Is The Everything Rally Vulnerable To Trump's New Tariffs?
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday announced new tariffs on dozens of countries, marking a resumption of the trade war policies that he originally outlined in April. Stocks fell around the world in reaction to the news, although all the major asset classes are still posting gains year to date, based on a set of ETFs through Thursday’s close (July 31). But with the return of an aggressive stance on trade from the White House, the near-term outlook for risk assets is again forced to price in the effects from erratic policy shifts.

As of yesterday’s 2025 performance data, the major market components around the world were posting gains across the board. The leading performer: developed-market stocks ex-US (VEA), which has rallied 19.1% so far this year. US shares (VTI), by comparison, are up a relatively moderate 8.0% in 2025. The Global Market Index (GMI), an unmanaged benchmark that holds all the major asset classes in market-value weights, is higher this year by nearly 10%.

The question now is how, or if, the return of trade war policies will affect markets. There’s certainly a lot of policy shift to digest, and so the near-term outlook will likely remain fluid.

For two of America’s largest trading partners – Mexico and Canada – sharply different rules apply. Mexico received a 90-day reprieve on new tariffs, allowing for negotiations to continue over the next three months. Canada, by contrast, is now subject to a hike in general tariffs to 35% from 25%.

By one estimate, Brazil has received the highest US tariff – 50%, according to analysis by the Financial Times. By contrast, the UK has the lowest tariff among America’s trading partners – a comparatively modest 10%.

The change in tariffs poses a new risk for the global economy, although economic activity has been relatively resilient in recent months following Trump’s initial announcement on tariffs in the spring. Investors will be closely watching how the latest update on US tariff policy affects expectations and incoming economic data.

“For the rest of the world, this is a serious demand shock,” Raghuram Rajan, former India central bank governor and chief economist of the International Monetary Fund, who is now a professor at the University of Chicago Booth School of Business, told Bloomberg TV today. “You will see a lot of central banks contemplating cutting as the rest of the world slows somewhat in the face of these tariffs.”

For the US, yesterday’s PCE inflation data for June hints at a possibility that pricing pressure is starting to creep higher. Prices rose 2.6% in June compared with a year ago, the Commerce Department said Thursday, edging up from an annual pace of 2.4% in May. Core PCE, which excludes the volatile food and energy categories, was steady at 2.8%.

“The Fed got validation [for leaving rates unchanged] this morning with inflation coming in a little higher than expected,” advised Scott Helfstein, head of investment strategy at Global X in an emailed comment. “There is some evidence of tariff impact in the inflation data, but it remains modest. Health care, housing and utilities continue to be key sources in driving inflation.”

The PCE inflation news was a factor in the latest pivot in Fed funds futures, which are now estimating a 61% probability that the central bank will leave its target rate unchanged at the next FOMC meeting in September. That compares with a modest estimate for a rate cut next month that had prevailed in recent days.

Perhaps the only certainty in the latest shift in tariff policy is that it’s expected to evolve further in the weeks and months ahead.

“Don’t assume this is the end of the story,” said Stephen Olson, Senior Visiting Fellow at ISEAS and a former US trade negotiator. “Trump regards this as an ongoing reality show. More ‘deals’ or further tariff increases are almost certain to follow. While we haven’t returned entirely to a ‘law of the jungle’ system, we have taken several huge strides back in that direction.”

data.description: The following slide deck was published by Alphatec Holdings, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Weak July Employment Report
data.description:
By David W. Berson, Ph.D.

A smaller-than-expected increase in nonfarm payrolls for July (only 73,000) and massive downward reductions in payrolls – lower by 238,000 for May and June. This is a huge revision to payrolls, and when coupled with the modestly below-market expectations for July, it suggests that the job market is substantially weaker than previously thought. Private sector employment increased a tad more than the total, up by 83,000 for July.

The U-3 unemployment rate edged higher to a still-low 4.2 percent – helped by both a small decline in the civilian labor force and a bigger rise in the number of unemployed workers.

These are not recessionary figures, but they are modest at best and show a deceleration in the job market. The advanced estimate for second quarter real GDP growth showed an acceleration to an annualized pace of 3.0 percent, but the "core" measure (real final sales to private domestic purchasers) slowed to an annualized pace of 1.2 percent – a bit below trend. The modest gain in July payrolls and the downward revisions to prior months support this.

Fed Chair Powell noted earlier this week that the Fed would continue to look at the data on inflation and the job market to determine future policy moves. The PCE inflation figures for July were a bit worse than expected, while the job figures were substantially worse. Does this suggest that the odds of near-term Fed easing have increased? The CME Group’s FedWatch tool shows there is a 67 percent chance that the Fed will ease by 25 basis points at the September FOMC meeting – almost a complete switch from yesterday when the odds were about 67 percent for no change. Moreover, there is now a nearly 25 percent chance that there will be three Fed cuts by year end.

If the inflation figures trend higher in the next couple of months (probably mostly from tariffs), then the Fed will face a difficult decision if the job market does not pick up. Should it stay the course (or even tighten policy) with higher prices, or should it respond to the weaker job market and ease policy? The answer is still not clear, but if the job market weakens further before the September FOMC meeting, Fed easing odds will rise further.

data.title: Agnico Eagle: Welcome To Hotel FreeCashflowrnia
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground. OP = open pit. Operating costs referred to in the 'Quarterly Production by Mine' section refer to cash costs.

Q2 Production & Sales

Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (NYSE:AEM) released its Q2'25 results this week, reporting quarterly production of ~866,000 ounces of gold, a 3% decline from the year-ago period. Meanwhile, gold sales were down 3% year-over-year as well to ~846,800 ounces, impacted by no contribution from La India/Creston Mascota (in residual leaching), a declining grade profile at Fosterville with the depletion of the Swan Zone (as expected), lower tonnes processed because of an extended caribou migration season in Nunavut, and some localized negative ore tonnage reconciliation at Detour Lake.

While the dip in production may be a disappointment to some investors newer to the story, it's important to put things in context.

For starters, Agnico Eagle's ("Agnico") production has been consistently growing relative to its major producer peers, up ~20x from the year 2000 (~174,000 ounces) while becoming a lower-risk and more battle-hardened business with nearly a dozen mines globally from one in 2000 (LaRonde). Two, production has more than doubled on a six-year basis vs. Q2'19 (2019 chosen because of pandemic impacts in 2020 that created easier comps to measure against), up from ~412,000 ounces to ~866,000 ounces. Three, Agnico Eagle is the only major that's accomplished this growth with glowing per share growth figures that include:

1. Growing its annual dividend by over 50x from $0.03 to $1.60.

2. Tripling its gold production per share.

3. Doubling its gold reserves per share.

These feats are even more impressive given that:

a] Gold isn't getting any easier to find, with many of the best deposits scooped up by other majors or mined out, and companies often having to trek deeper below the surface or into more difficult terrains to add ounces and/or make new discoveries

b] Ounces in the ground aren't getting any easier to buy because of competition, especially with a growth-for-growth's sake mentality among most producers in the sector

c] Agnico hasn't sacrificed on its core strategy to accomplish this growth—building/operating world-class assets with a regional focus in top-ranked jurisdictions

Given these challenges and unwillingness to settle, the unrivaled per-share growth can be attributed to three things, among others.

1) Buying high-quality ounces at prices well below fair value in jurisdictions that it knows well, ideally with strong currency [outperforming shares] or in periods of commodity price weakness

2) Adding value through the drillbit to replace reserves, extend lives, and maintain above-average grades at these world-class assets

3) Never sacrificing jurisdiction, which can lead to negative surprises and defeat its goal of multiple mines over multiple decades (even if it succeeds on the exploration front), because of factors that may be outside of its control

Moving back to Q2'25 production, the other point worth noting is that lower production and sales were related to lapping tough comps overall, with Q2'24 being the 2nd best quarter in the company's history from a production standpoint (~895,800 ounces), helped by an exceptional performance from Canadian Malartic (~180,900 ounces, 1.17 G/T of gold processed) and Meadowbank (~126,400 ounces, 4.36 G/T of gold processed), and a ~6,100 ounce headwind with La India not contributing in Q2'25.

On a positive note, the slight dip in Q2'25 production hasn't derailed Agnico's ability to meet guidance. In fact, production is sitting at ~51% of its 3.4 million ounce guidance midpoint and looks set to surpass its guidance midpoint for a third consecutive year after solid beats in 2023 and 2024. So, unlike its major producer peers where the below lyrics better apply, Agnico is consistently delivering on promises, and 2025 doesn't look any different.

'You can cut guidance any time you like,

but you can never meet'

Perhaps most impressive, though, is the fact that Agnico is also tracking well against its cost guidance despite the impact of higher royalties. In fact, FY2025 AISC is sitting below its guidance midpoint of $1,275/oz, with the potential for Agnico to beat on costs as well this year. Plus, while others are seeing a meaningful decline in production from Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions, like Newmont Corporation (NEM), Agnico is actually seeing growth thanks to two-well timed deals (Kirkland Lake Gold/Yamana Canada), with Tier-1 jurisdiction coming in at 98% of total production (up 1,000 basis points from 2020 levels), over double that of Newmont at 44% in Q2'25, down ~2,000 basis points from 2023 levels.

Let's dig into the quarter a little closer below and check out production from some of its key operations:

Quarterly Production by Mine

Detour Lake

Starting with its massive Detour Lake Mine (700,000+ ounces/year) in Ontario, production came in at ~168,300 ounces at $914/oz, with production flat year-over-year albeit at higher costs (Q2'24: $791/oz). On a positive note, it was a record second quarter for milled tonnes at Detour Lake with ~6.84 million tonnes processed at 0.85 G/T of gold, beating the ~6.79 million tonnes in the year-ago period. This continues to reinforce the potential for this asset to operate closer to 30 MTPA and support even higher production rates.

On a negative note, production is sitting at just ~44.6% of its guidance midpoint (~321,100 ounces of planned 720,000 ounces), with Agnico sharing that production is likely to be at the lower end of the full year range (705,000 to 735,000 ounces). This is related to mining that's been focused on a lower-grade domain with some localized negative ore tonnage reconciliation. Unfortunately, mining will remain in this low-grade domain in Q3, but Agnico noted grades should step up in Q4'25 towards a range of 0.97 - 1.0 G/T of gold.

Assuming ~6.5 million tonnes processed in Q4'25 (planned major shutdown in Q4 for mill maintenance), an average grade of 0.98 G/T of gold and 91% recoveries, this should allow for a much better Q4'25 at ~186,000 ounces, with production set to end the year on a strong note. Looking ahead though, Agnico firmly believes that this asset can be a future 1.0 million ounce producer with the addition of an underground component to complement its massive open-pit, with Agnico wasting no time on this opportunity.

In fact, the first blast for the exploration ramp occurred in early July to begin construction of the ramp towards the West Extension Zone, where the company hopes to extract a bulk sample in H1-27.

Canadian Malartic

Moving to Canadian Malartic, it was another impressive quarter from this asset with ~172,500 ounces produced at $876/oz, albeit down from a massive quarter in Q2'24 with ~180,900 ounces produced at $871/oz. The strong production was helped by better grades than planned in the Barnat Pit with mining zones near historical UG stopes, and ~4.96 million tonnes were processed in Q2'25 at 1.17 G/T of gold. Overall, this has left Canadian Malartic sitting at ~56.3% of its guidance midpoint (~332,300 ounces vs. full-year guidance of 590,000 ounces).

In terms of Malartic Underground or Odyssey, Agnico reported record quarterly production of ~26,600 ounces of gold and record tonnes mined of ~3,960 TPD, trouncing its target of 3,500 TPD. As for progress on the Odyssey Project, the company shared that surface construction is on schedule and budget, with construction of the second phase of the paste plant (20k TPD) underway and the fabrication of the production hoist ongoing in Germany. As for development, it hit a record of 4,850 meters in Q2'25, and the ramp reached the mid-shaft loading station at L102, with East Gouldie production remaining on track for H2'26.

Agnico also shared that breakthrough of the shaft is scheduled for Q3'25, the main ramp towards the shaft bottom is at a depth of 1,019 meters and the shaft is sitting at a depth of 1,179 meters (of a planned 1,800 meters), with conventional shaft sinking set to resume this quarter, ahead of schedule. It also noted that it is contemplating a 70-meter extension of this shaft to 1,870 meters that could improve operational flexibility and efficiency, further reduce reliance on truck haulage and allow it to better attack the exploration upside at depth. According to commentary in the Q2'25 Call, this extension could be implemented for a very modest capex of ~$40 million or what is barely four days of free cash flow generation at spot prices.

Macassa

As for its Macassa Mine in Ontario, production came in at a new record of ~87,400 ounces, helped by localized positive grade reconciliation and a change in mine sequencing. This was evidenced by industry-leading grades of 19.5 G/T of gold milled in Q2'25 with throughput of ~143,000 tonnes, contributing to a near 37% increase in production year-over-year at lower costs ($626/oz vs. $833/oz). Given the strong H1'25 with grades well above plan (291,000 tonnes processed at 18.99 G/T of gold vs. budget of 14.15 G/T for full-year 2025), it wouldn't be surprising to see a dip in grades in H2'25.

Still, Macassa is tracking extremely well against its guidance midpoint, sitting at ~55.9%, with a minor contribution (up to 10,000 ounces) expected from the shallower AK Zone (being sent to LZ5 mill in Q4-25).

Fosterville

Moving over to its other high-grade mine, Fosterville, this asset is still chugging after years of pushing out 600,000 ounces/year, which exhausted its highest grade Swan Zone in reserves. And like Macassa, which enjoyed positive grade reconciliation, Fosterville also had a better quarter than expected from a grade standpoint, processing ~188,000 tonnes at 8.52 G/T of gold (Q2'24: ~234,000 tonnes at 9.06 G/T of gold) for ~49,600 ounces produced at $783/oz costs. Agnico noted grades were anticipated at Robbins Hill/Harrier, with grades also better than expected at Phoenix due to a change in mining sequence.

Given the better-than-budgeted grades year-to-date, Fosterville is sitting at ~62.1% of its guidance midpoint on the back of YTD grades of 8.57 G/T of gold vs. expectations of 5.83 G/T of gold this year. Like Macassa, this suggests a dip in grades in H2'25, but Fosterville is still tracking comfortably against its guidance midpoint. In terms of the future of this asset, work is ongoing to increase production towards 175,000 ounces by 2027 (new goal), driven by higher mining rates in Lower Phoenix. In terms of progress, the upgrade of the primary ventilation system with commissioning of primary fans is now scheduled for Q4'25.

Meliadine

As for its Meliadine Mine in Nunavut, production came in at ~90,300 ounces at $1,112/oz costs, up from ~88,700 ounces at $892/oz in the year-ago period. This was based on ~545,000 tonnes processed at 5.32 G/T of gold, benefiting from its mill expansion (plans to go to 6.5k TPD in 2026), albeit with fewer tonnes processed than planned. Lower production was related to 11 days of caribou migration vs. 7 days budgeted and an unplanned mill shut in Q2, impacting ore haulage despite record development in April that contributed to significantly higher development than planned in Q2'25.

Given the negative impact of the longer-than-planned migration period, Meliadine is sitting at ~49% of its guidance midpoint with ~188,800 ounces produced year-to-date.

Meadowbank

As for Meadowbank, it was affected much more heavily by the longer caribou migration period, and while there's lots of wildlife in the vicinity of the site ranging from wolverines to wolves, the caribou, while friendlier, have the most impact on Agnico's Arctic operations. During Q2'25, Agnico shared it had road closures between Amaruq (mine site) and Meadowbank (mill site) and a mill shut lasting 27 days, well above the 27 days and 9 days budgeted, which obviously affected mill throughput. The result was gold production of ~101,900 ounces at $1,018/oz costs, down from tough comparisons of ~146,400 ounces at $922/oz costs in Q2'24.

Although the setback is unfortunate during a period of record gold prices, the gold isn't going anywhere. In fact, any technical work around a potential mine-life expansion at Amaruq is certainly supported by these gold prices with the potential to extend the life of this asset into the 2030s. In terms of the most recent commentary around this, that would allow for a smoother production profile vs. potentially losing this ~500,000 ounce/year asset later this decade (almost fully replaced by Hope Bay in early 2030s at 400,000 ounces, but still a minor net dip in production). Agnico noted it could extend Amaruq to 2034 at a lower rate, enabling that smoother production profile.

Assuming this were the case, this would give ample time for three of its ex-Nunavut growth projects (Malartic Fill The Fill, Detour UG, Upper Beaver) to be at or near steady state with far less of a production impact from Meadowbank going offline if it can be extended to 2033/2034.

Kittila

Looking at Kittila in Finland, production came in at ~50,400 ounces at $1,134/oz costs, down slightly from ~55,700 ounces at $1,020/oz costs in Q2'24. However, production costs per tonne were actually lower year-over-year in both Q2'25 and H1'25, lending to continuous improvement initiatives that drove down costs. These included the internalization of work previously done by contractors and hauling waste rock through the shaft. As for the H2-25 outlook, Kittila processed ~1.0 million tonnes at 3.92 G/T of gold in H1'25 vs. an annual budget of ~2.05 million tonnes at 4.15 G/T of gold, implying a better H2'25 if in line with plans with slightly higher grades.

During Q2'25, production was impacted by an 11-day shut of its autoclave for scheduled maintenance, affecting throughput in the period (~482,000 tonnes processed).

LaRonde/Goldex

Last but not least, LaRonde and Goldex. Starting with LaRonde production came in at ~91,300 ounces at $807/oz costs, up sharply from the year-ago period. This was related to higher grades, benefiting from positive grade reconciliation in three stopes at the East Mine, West Mine and 11-3 Zone. Meanwhile, LZ5 performed above budget (3.63k TPD vs. 3.5k TPD), with continued progress on its automation targets and 24% of ore hauled to the surface using automated scoops/trucks.

Notably, Agnico teased in its Q2'25 Call that automation could be an opportunity for the company and that LZ5 is its test mine to ultimately be able to implement its automation initiatives and new technology at other sites like Macassa. In terms of opportunities, the company is looking to pilot a fleet management system underground, potentially yielding up to 15% improvement and while this may not seem like much for a small site like LZ5 with ~40,000 ounces of production year-to-date, leveraging this technology at larger UG sites could be a game-changer for the company.

Plus, not only could this mean higher productivity, less downtime and improved operations in general with short interval control to catch/improve operations in real-time, but those productivity gains could have other positive impacts. One potential gain could be a lower hurdle for reserve replacement and conversion because if costs per tonne are lower due to productivity gains, cut-off grades could potentially be relaxed slightly, resulting in a lower hurdle rate for replacing reserves each year. This is all very early stage today, but it's positive to see the gains already made at LZ5 and the potential for a lot more to come with automation/technology and AI.

Finally, as for Goldex, it reported record tonnage in Q2'25 at ~819,000 tonnes with record tonnes processed from the satellite Akasaba West site, another solid quarter for this Quebec operation.

Financial Results

As for Agnico's financial results, they were phenomenal, to say the least. Revenue came in at ~$2.82 billion (+35% year-over-year), operating cash flow soared to ~$1.85 billion (+92% year-over-year) and free cash flow increased 134% to ~$1.31 billion or US$2.60/share, or a 134% increase year-over-year. It's important to note that this was partially related to tax deferrals, with a large chunk of taxes related to this year's profitability to be paid in Q1-26. That said, this was still a record quarter for free cash flow and free cash flow per share even netting out this impact, with free cash flow before changes in non-cash components of working capital of ~$792 million or US$1.58/share.

In terms of free cash flow generation this year, Agnico should generate upwards of $4.0 billion in free cash flow, helping it to grow into its valuation with it trading at ~16x FY2025 free cash flow estimates at a share price of US$127.00. Not surprisingly, this has allowed the company to improve its already strong balance sheet, flipping into a strong net cash position at quarter-end, with ~$960 million in net cash after paying down $550 million in debt and reducing its debt position to $595 million.

And given that the gold price continues to levitate near record prices, Agnico is in a position where it could potentially deploy another $300 million towards buybacks in H2'25 and still end the year with over $2.0 billion in net cash, giving it a war chest to use on either opportunistic M&A, further share buybacks and/or a stockpile to prepare for its large suite of growth projects. The good news in Agnico's case, though, is that it doesn't need to make any acquisitions given its strong pipeline and can instead be patient for the right opportunities given that it took advantage in weaker portions of the cycle.

Costs & Margins

Moving to costs and margins, Agnico's cost control is evidenced by the chart below, highlighting its ability to maintain costs at/around the $1,200/oz despite inflationary pressures felt sector-wide since 2020. This can partially be attributed to its competitive advantage with a regional model and its lower contractor/employee ratio vs. peers, but also to its track record of laser-focused operational execution that has ensured as much margin as possible flows to its shareholders. As evidence of this, AISC came in at industry-leading levels at $1,289/oz (+10% year-over-year), and year-to-date AISC is sitting at $1,235 (+5% year-over-year), well on track to meet and potentially beat its guidance midpoint of $1,275/oz.

Combined with a record gold price of $3,288/oz in Q2'25, this translated to AISC margins of $2,019/oz or 61.4%, up 1,130 basis points year-over-year from $1,173/oz or 50.1%. Notably, this was despite higher sustaining capital spend (~$234 million vs. ~$205 million) and unlike Newmont that saw better than expected AISC on under-investment in H1'25 in planned sustaining capital (deferred capital to H2'25 which made all-in sustaining costs look much better short-term), this is not the case for Agnico Eagle which is tracking closer to budget (over 46% of planned annual spend in H1 vs. 42% for Newmont).

Recent Developments

Canadian Malartic (Odyssey) Upside

Digging into Canadian Malartic, there's been no shortage of exploration success at this asset and the impressive hits keep on coming. Recent results include 36.2 meters at 3.4 G/T of gold at 1,947 meters depth and 12.9 meters at 3.5 G/T of gold at 1,993 meters depth, plus 19.2 meters at 3.5 G/T of gold at 2,013 meters depth. These are the deepest holes drilled to date, confirming mineralization at even lower depths than its hits in Q1-25 (27.4 meters at 5.3 G/T of gold at 1,840 meter depths, 17.7 meters at 6.6 G/T of gold at 1,886 meter depths). Importantly, these hits continue to come at very mineable widths and don't appear to be pinching at depth, suggesting room for material resource growth.

As for hits in the Upper East extension of East Gouldie, Agnico hit 17.7 meters at 5.7 G/T of gold near ramp/shaft infrastructure, pointing to reserve growth in close proximity to existing infrastructure and complementing prior hits of 17.3 meters at 3.7 G/T of gold at slightly shallower depths in Q1-25. Finally, the new Eclipse discovery yielded a solid hit of 7.2 meters at 4.3 G/T of gold (1,507 meter depth) and 14.0 meters at 3.8 G/T of gold (1,519 meter depth), with Agnico noting this zone has the potential to add "significant" resources near-mine.

Hope Bay

Moving north to Hope Bay, this is another asset that keeps on giving, and the company has put on a masterclass in how to make accretive acquisitions, paying less than $400 million for this multi-million ounce resource with infrastructure in 2021 while TMac struggled to operate profitably at Doris. However, after two years of drilling at Doris, Agnico has switched its focus to Madrid and Suluk/Patch 7 where it believes it can restart the operation with higher grades in the early years.

To date, aggressively drilling is certainly supporting this, with more high-grade hits in Q2'25 that included 8.4 meters at 25.7 G/T of gold just north of an inferred resource block at depth (~750 meters), 3.2 meters at 7.4 G/T of gold just below and south of its deepest indicated resource block at Patch 7 and 4.4 meters at 16.1 G/T of gold in shallower infill drilling, also at Patch 7. Meanwhile, shallower drilling in the Patch 7/Suluk gap hit 10.8 meters at 4.4 G/T of gold and 3.5 meters at 6.5 G/T of gold. And while not as high-grade, this appears to be confirming the presence of economic mineralization at much shallower depths.

In terms of development here, the Naartok East exploration ramp advanced by 482 meters of a planned 2.1 kilometers and is now at a depth of 38 meters at quarter-end. In addition, the southern extension of the gravel track running south of Patch Lake was completed on schedule last quarter, reducing helicopter costs and providing better access to southern targets, Patch 14 and Wolverine. Finally, the company has begun the dismantling of major components of the existing Doris Mill (built/operated by TMac).

Last but not least, Agnico Eagle shared that it is supporting the Nunavut 3000 housing initiative with a goal to build 3,000 new homes in Nunavut by 2030 to support the growing population (~40,000 currently) and its short-term goal of 1,000 homes by October of this year. As noted by the company, it signed an MOU to ship 20 new modular homes to Rankin Inlet and Baker Lake to help chip away at the near-term 1,000 new home target coming up by year-end, with the potential to extend this agreement to future years.

Investment Portfolio

As for Agnico's investment portfolio, it may have ~$960 million in net cash but thanks to a more ebullient general market environment for precious metals stocks and well-selected names, it's now sitting on over $1.0 billion in equity securities as well. Larger positions include Rupert Resources Ltd. (OTCQX:RUPRF), Orla Mining Ltd. (ORLA), and Foran Mining Corporation (OTCQX:FMCXF), and while Agnico didn't specify and remained vague, it did note that it is a lot of money, suggesting it may look to re-balance this portfolio a little at some point given the significant move in its holdings (near $400 million gain year-to-date).
