Share Buybacks and Dividends

In terms of returning capital to shareholders, Agnico has opted for share buybacks over dividends temporarily, with ~836,500 shares repurchased in Q2'25 at US$119.47 and commentary that it could be busier on its buyback program in H2'25. This choice to favor buybacks over an increase in its base dividend isn't overly surprising given that the company has historically been very conservative when raising its base dividend to ensure it does not need to cut. As some may remember, it was forced to cut after a sharp reversal in the gold price in early 2014 to $0.08/quarter vs. $0.22/quarter just after it had raised by 10% at the start of 2013.

Although some are disappointed with the lack of a dividend increase after a $1,200/oz+ move in the gold price, the keyword as part of its dividend policy lies in the below sentence, which is sustainable.

The Company's goal is to increase its dividend to shareholders, over time, on a sustainable basis.

I believe the decision to wait and see in terms of dividend increases is the responsible move, especially with the track record of dividends in this sector, and the fact that one can build up a track record over decades but it takes very little to tear down that trust. That said, and while Agnico hasn't guaranteed anything at this time (given that the gold price is obviously not in its control and only time will tell where the new floor is), I would not be surprised to see the dividend raised in its year-end 2025 results to $1.72/share to $1.80/share which would be ~$860 million to ~$900 million per year or a near $100 million increase.

In further defense of the company's choice to stand pat on its dividend, it is currently in an investment phase and will remain in an investment phase if several mid-scale and large projects are approved (Detour UG, Canadian Malartic Second Shaft, Upper Beaver, San Nicolas 50%, Hope Bay, Wasamac), with Agnico having an extremely busy pipeline. This incredible organic growth profile is a great 'problem' to have from a capex standpoint. However, it also means that dividend increases should be moderate and conservative just in case the gold price were to change gears in a big way in this period of higher investment.

To summarize, with a near flawless track record of over-delivering on promises that has taken years to solidify, there's no need to take on that risk, with it being easy to spoil a great reputation short-term with a minor misstep whereas share buybacks can be stopped and started as pleased and might be a lower risk way to return capital short-term while it waits to see where the new range for the gold price settles. The other option, of course, is a special dividend to supplement share buybacks, but I don't think a high single-digit increase in the base dividend is unreasonable at all, and I wouldn't be surprised to see something announced by Q2'26.

Valuation

Based on ~504 million fully diluted shares and a share price of US$127.00, Agnico trades at a market cap of ~$64.0 billion and an enterprise value of ~$63.0 billion. This continues to leave it as one of the highest valued names in the sector with a premium multiple vs. peers, as shown below. However, this distinction is more than justified given its unrivaled track record of per share growth, impressive capital discipline and consistent operational execution with a penchant for over-delivering on its goals from both a production and resource/reserve growth standpoint.

The latter is certainly helped by Agnico consistently being one of the most aggressive drillers sector-wide, but it doesn't hurt to have world-class ore bodies and a guiding light in Guy Gosselin (EVP Exploration) leading his exploration, with multiple major discoveries under their belt, including Amaruq and Odyssey Underground. Just as important as the pace of resource growth and reserve replacement, though, is that these ounces are being added in Tier-1-ranked jurisdictions that are safe to mine in, governed by the rule of law, and with relatively predictable permitting timelines.

Ultimately, this allows for low-risk deployment of capital in new projects (DL-UG, Malartic Fill-The-Mill) and/or new mine builds/restarts (Upper Beaver, Hope Bay) when you have the confidence that you can mine there over decades. And combined with an executive team that is laser-focused on risk-adjusted returns over absolute returns, this largely explains the stock's premium multiple, especially in a sector where there are only a handful of Tier-1 only producers with scale.

Summary

Agnico Eagle put together another solid quarter in Q2-25 and is now sitting at ~51% of its guidance midpoint despite a longer than planned caribou migration season and slightly lower production at Detour Lake. More important than production, though, the company has done an exceptional job of keeping a lid on costs, maintaining its original cost guidance despite the impact of higher royalties (gold price related), ensuring shareholders capture as much excess margin as possible. However, perhaps most important is the fact that its project pipeline has never been stronger, with a suite of projects that most producers would salivate for, focused primarily on organic growth in top-ranked jurisdictions, with some of these opportunities having the potential to create 1.0+ million-ounce mines.

This enviable position and its industry-leading per share metrics are thanks to disciplined growth over the past decade, planting seeds in the weaker part of the cycle while many others floundered or overpaid for growth, and success at the drillbit plus optimization work that's morphed great assets into world-class assets. Hence, while investors are already getting a glimpse of what this free cash flow machine is capable of in a metals bull market, there's much more to come with the potential for production closer to 4.0 million ounces, with 50% of that coming from two million-ounce assets with industry-leading margins.

The result is the potential for $4.8+ billion in annual free cash flow early next decade (~US$9.60/share) even at just a $3,200/oz gold price assumption. At $3,500/oz gold, this figure jumps to ~$5.4 billion or (US$10.80) in free cash flow per share, pointing to significant long-term upside given that a business of this caliber can easily justify trading at 20x free cash flow.

Given this solid positioning coupled with the fact that it's a company that has proven it can execute, Agnico Eagle remains one of the most attractive ways to get exposure to the gold sector. Hence, I continue to see it as a core name for a precious metals-focused portfolio, and I would view any sharp pullbacks in the stock as buying opportunities.
